summary: A summary of Hildegard of Bingen's theological vision as presented in translations of her works, particularly 'The Book of Divine Works'. It highlights her belief in the deep interconnectedness of the cosmos and humanity, the centrality of the Incarnation, and the human calling to cooperate in God's ongoing creation.

principles.statement: The Incarnation is the key moment of all history.
principles.rationale: It was willed from eternity to complete God's Work, sharing God's creative capacity and loving mission with humanity.

principles.statement: Humans contain all creation within themselves and are called to cooperate in the Creator's work.
principles.rationale: Because humans are made in God's image and share in the Word's creative rationality, they are divinely called to maintain a fruitful and sustainable relationship with creation.

principles.statement: God's self-revelation is simultaneously cosmic and intimately close.
principles.rationale: The Work of God extends from the infinite down into the smallest details of the created world, making the divine presence both grand and deeply personal.

mental_models.name: The Universal Man (Microcosm and Macrocosm)
mental_models.description: A lens viewing the human person as intricately connected to the physical and spiritual cosmos, visualized as a human figure standing astride the cosmic spheres, containing all creation within themselves.

mental_models.name: The Geography of Salvation
mental_models.description: Imagining the history of salvation and moral consequences through spatial metaphors, such as the City of God standing next to the mountain of God's foreknowledge, or mapping virtues and vices onto a geography of purgatory, hellmouth, and the heavenly city.

mental_models.name: Cosmic Effects of Sin
mental_models.description: A theological framework where human morality is not isolated but woven into the fabric of the universe; human sin has cosmic effects, and the restoration of order is achieved through Christ.
